subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : The property of body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original shape when the external force is removed, is Elasticity.
Reason (R) : The restoring force depends upon the bonded inter atomic and inter molecular force of solid.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : The property of body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original shape when the external force is removed, is Elasticity.
Reason (R) : The restoring force depends upon the bonded inter atomic and inter molecular force of solid.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Elasticity is the ability of a material to deform under stress and then return to its original shape when the stress is removed.

The restoring force in elastic materials is associated with the inter-atomic or inter-molecular forces that act to bring the atoms or molecules back to their original positions when the material is deformed. When an external force is applied to a material, it causes a displacement of atoms or molecules from their equilibrium positions. The restoring force is a result of the potential energy stored in these inter-atomic or inter-molecular bonds, and it works to bring the material back to its original state.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)

<math> <mrow> <mfenced close="]" open="[" separators=","> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <msub> <mtext mathvariant="italic">T</mtext> <mtext mathvariant="italic">M</mtext> </msub> </mrow> <mrow> <mtext mathvariant="italic">T</mtext> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </mfenced> <mfrac> <mrow> <mtext mathvariant="italic">Mg</mtext> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>
solution:
Initial length =l. Time period T=2πlg        ...(i). After suspending mass M,. Youngs modulus Y=StressStrain=FA∆ll=Fl∆l A

Change in length of wire Δl=FlAY

Now time period TM=2πl+Δlg  ...(ii)       

TTM=2πlg2πl+Δlg        ∵ i ii

T2TM2=ll+Δl

⇒Tm2T2=1+∆ll
1+FAY=TmT2putting value of ∆l
1Y=TmT2-1AF
F=Mg
1Y=TmT2-1AMg

subject: Physics
q: Two persons pull a wire towards themselves. Each person exerts a force of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ on the wire. Young's modulus of the material of wire is $1 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$. Original length of the wire is $2 \mathrm{~m}$ and the area of cross section is $2 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. The wire will extend in length by ______ $\mu \mathrm{m}$.
question: Two persons pull a wire towards themselves. Each person exerts a force of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ on the wire. Young's modulus of the material of wire is $1 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$. Original length of the wire is $2 \mathrm{~m}$ and the area of cross section is $2 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. The wire will extend in length by ______ $\mu \mathrm{m}$.
solution:
$200 \mathrm{~N} \longleftrightarrow O$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{Y} \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} \Rightarrow \Delta \ell=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{AY}} \\ & \Delta \ell=\frac{200 \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{11}}=2 \times 10^{-5}=20 \mu \mathrm{m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of cross sectional area A, modulus of elasticity $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is stretched between two vertical rigid supports. When a mass of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended at the middle it sags lower from its original position making angle $\theta=\frac{1}{100}$ radian on the points of support. The value of $\mathrm{A}$ is _______ $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ (consider $x< < \mathrm{L}$ ). (given : $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
question: A wire of cross sectional area A, modulus of elasticity $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is stretched between two vertical rigid supports. When a mass of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended at the middle it sags lower from its original position making angle $\theta=\frac{1}{100}$ radian on the points of support. The value of $\mathrm{A}$ is _______ $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ (consider $x< < \mathrm{L}$ ). (given : $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
solution: In vertical direction $2 \mathrm{~T} \sin \theta=20$ using small angle approximation $\sin \theta=\theta$ $\begin{aligned} & \theta=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{10}{\theta} \\ & \mathrm{T}=1000 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{array}{ll} \text {Change in length } \Delta \mathrm{L} =2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{L}^2}-2 \mathrm{~L} \\ =2 \mathrm{~L}\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2 \mathrm{~L}^2}-1\right] \\ \Delta \mathrm{L} =\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~L}} \end{array}$ $\therefore$ Modulus of elasticity $=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}$ $\begin{aligned} & 2 \times 10^{11}=\frac{10^3}{\mathrm{~A} \times \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~L}}} \times 2 \mathrm{~L} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{A}=1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Two wires as shown in the figure below, made of steel and have breaking stress of $12 \times 10^8$ N/m$^2$. Area of cross-section of upper wire is $0.008$ cm$^2$ and of lower wire is $0.004$ cm$^2$. The maximum mass that can be added to pan without breaking any wire is _____ kg.
(take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question:
Two wires as shown in the figure below, made of steel and have breaking stress of $12 \times 10^8$ N/m$^2$. Area of cross-section of upper wire is $0.008$ cm$^2$ and of lower wire is $0.004$ cm$^2$. The maximum mass that can be added to pan without breaking any wire is _____ kg.
(take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Let the maximum mass that can be added to the pan be $m$.

The breaking stress for both wires is $\sigma_{max} = 12 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}^2$.

For the upper wire:
Area of cross-section, $A_u = 0.008 \text{ cm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$
Maximum tension the upper wire can withstand is:
$T_{u,max} = \sigma_{max} \times A_u = (12 \times 10^8) \times (8 \times 10^{-7}) = 960 \text{ N}$
The total mass supported by the upper wire is $(30 + 10 + m) = (40 + m) \text{ kg}$.
Tension in the upper wire, $T_u = (40 + m)g = (40 + m) \times 10$
To avoid breaking the upper wire:
$(40 + m) \times 10 \le 960$
$40 + m \le 96 \implies m \le 56 \text{ kg}$

For the lower wire:
Area of cross-section, $A_l = 0.004 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$
Maximum tension the lower wire can withstand is:
$T_{l,max} = \sigma_{max} \times A_l = (12 \times 10^8) \times (4 \times 10^{-7}) = 480 \text{ N}$
The total mass supported by the lower wire is $(10 + m) \text{ kg}$.
Tension in the lower wire, $T_l = (10 + m)g = (10 + m) \times 10$
To avoid breaking the lower wire:
$(10 + m) \times 10 \le 480$
$10 + m \le 48 \implies m \le 38 \text{ kg}$

To ensure neither wire breaks, the added mass $m$ must satisfy both conditions. Therefore, the maximum mass that can be added is the smaller of the two values:
$m = 38 \text{ kg}$

Answer: $38$

If the bulk modulus of cork is B and the coefficient of friction between the bottle and cork is μ, then the force needed to push the cork into the bottle is

⇒N=B4πb△a

f=μN=μ4πbBΔa

subject: Physics
q: A steel wire of length 2 m and Young's modulus $2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ is stretched by a force. If Poisson ratio and transverse strain for the wire are 0.2 and $10^{-3}$ respectively, then the elastic potential energy density of the wire is _______ $\times 10^5$ (in SI units)
question: A steel wire of length 2 m and Young's modulus $2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ is stretched by a force. If Poisson ratio and transverse strain for the wire are 0.2 and $10^{-3}$ respectively, then the elastic potential energy density of the wire is _______ $\times 10^5$ (in SI units)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \ell=2 \mathrm{~m} ; \mathrm{Y}=2 \times 10^{11} \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~m}^2} \\ & \begin{aligned} \mu=-\frac{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right)}{\left(\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell}\right)} \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} & =\frac{1}{\mu} \times\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{0.2} \times\left(10^{-3}\right)\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell}=5 \times 10^{-3} \\ & \begin{aligned} \mathrm{u}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{y} \varepsilon_{\ell}^2 & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times\left[5 \times 10^{-3}\right]^2 \\ & =25\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

solution:
Elongation due to self weight,
Wl2AY=10×0.22×2×1011×100×10-4=5×10-10 m

q: The bulk moduli of ethanol, mercury and water are given as $0.9,25$ and $2.2$ respectively in units of $10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. For a given value of pressure, the fractional compression in volume is $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}$. Which of the following statements about $\frac{\Delta V}{V}$ for these three liquids is correct ?
question: The bulk moduli of ethanol, mercury and water are given as $0.9,25$ and $2.2$ respectively in units of $10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. For a given value of pressure, the fractional compression in volume is $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}$. Which of the following statements about $\frac{\Delta V}{V}$ for these three liquids is correct ?
solution:
Compressibility $=\frac{1}{\text { Bulk modulus }}$
As bulk modulus is least for ethanol (0.9) and maximum for mercury (25) among ehtanol, mercury and water. Hence compression in volume $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}$ Ethanol $>$ Water $>$ Mercury

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion (A) : Steel is used in the construction of buildings and bridges.
Reason (R) : Steel is more elastic and its elastic limit is high. 

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion (A) : Steel is used in the construction of buildings and bridges.
Reason (R) : Steel is more elastic and its elastic limit is high. 

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct
solution:
The relationship between Young's modulus, stress, and strain is:

stressstrain=Y

Since the elastic modulus of steel is more, for the same amount of stress, the strain will be less resulting in less deformation of buildings and bridges. Therefore, steel is used for construction of large structures. 

Hence, both assertion and reason are correct and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

solution:
The data given is

Bulk modulus is given by,

=-VddVaV3

=-V-3aV4

=3aV3=3P

q:
A uniform heavy rod of mass 20 kg. Cross sectional area 0.4 m2 and length 20 m is hanging from a fixed support. Neglecting the lateral contraction, the elongation in the rod due to its own weight is x×10-9 m. The value of x is _____ .
(Given. Young's modulus Y=2×1011Nm-2 and g=10 m s-2 )
question:
A uniform heavy rod of mass 20 kg. Cross sectional area 0.4 m2 and length 20 m is hanging from a fixed support. Neglecting the lateral contraction, the elongation in the rod due to its own weight is x×10-9 m. The value of x is _____ .
(Given. Young's modulus Y=2×1011Nm-2 and g=10 m s-2 )
solution:
Tension at a distance x from lower end =mglx

If the elongation in the element is taken as d∆l then, using Hooke's law,

Y=mgxdxAld∆l⇒d∆l=maxdxAlY⇒∫0∆ld∆l=∫0lmgxdxAlY⇒∆l=mgl2AY

⇒Δl=20×10×202×0.4×2×1011

⇒Δl=25×10-9

⇒x=25

q: A rod, of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> at room temperature and uniform area of cross section <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is made of a metal having coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo> </mo><mo>/</mo><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> It is observed that an external compressive force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is applied on each of its ends, prevents any change in the length of the rod when its temperature rises by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>T</mi><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mn>K</mn></math> Young's modulus, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> for this metal is:
question: A rod, of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> at room temperature and uniform area of cross section <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is made of a metal having coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo> </mo><mo>/</mo><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> It is observed that an external compressive force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is applied on each of its ends, prevents any change in the length of the rod when its temperature rises by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>T</mi><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mn>K</mn></math> Young's modulus, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> for this metal is:
solution:
Since the length of the rod does not change, the increment due to increase in temperature will be balanced by the compression due to applied force.

Y=FAΔll  ⇒ Δll=FAY

Also Δll=αΔT

∴  FAY=α ΔT

Y=FAαΔT

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long wire having cross-sectional area  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the elongation in the wire will be :

solution:
As we know, elongation in wire is given by,

∆l=FlAY

⇒∆l=250×1006.25×10-4×1010

⇒∆l=4×10-3 m

q: Speed of a transverse wave on a straight wire (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo></math> length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>  is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the Young's modulus of wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the extension of wire over its natural length is:
question: Speed of a transverse wave on a straight wire (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo></math> length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>  is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the Young's modulus of wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the extension of wire over its natural length is:
solution:
v=Tμ
⇒T=μv2
⇒μv2A=Y∆ll
⇒∆l=μv2lAY
After substituting value of μ,v,l,A and Y we get
∆l=0.3mm

subject: Physics
q: If average depth of an ocean is 4000 m and the bulk modulus of water is 2×109 N m-2, then fractional compression ΔVV of water at the bottom of ocean is α×10-2. The value of α is _______, (Given, g=10 m s-2, ρ=1000 kg m-3)
question: If average depth of an ocean is 4000 m and the bulk modulus of water is 2×109 N m-2, then fractional compression ΔVV of water at the bottom of ocean is α×10-2. The value of α is _______, (Given, g=10 m s-2, ρ=1000 kg m-3)
solution:
The formula to calculate the Bulk modulus is given by

B=-∆P∆VV   ...1

From equation (1), the fractional compression in volume may be obtained as follows:

-ΔVV=∆PB=ρg∆hB   ...2

From equation (2) it follows that

-∆VV=1000×10×40002×109⇒∆VV=2×10-2

Hence, α=2.

subject: Physics
q: A steel wire having a radius of <math><mn>2.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , carrying a load of <math><mn>4</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is hanging from a ceiling. Given that <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> what will be the tensile stress that would be developed in the wire?
question: A steel wire having a radius of <math><mn>2.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , carrying a load of <math><mn>4</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is hanging from a ceiling. solution:
Stress =mgπr2=4×3.1×ππ×2×10-32
=3.1×106N/m2

question:
Two blocks of masses 3 kg and 5 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley. The breaking stress of the metal is 24π×102 N m-2. What is the minimum radius of the wire ?
( take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Tension in the string =2m1m2m1+m2g

=2×3×5×108=752

Stress =TA

24π×102=752πr2

r2=7548×10-2

r2=0.015625

r=0.125 m=12.5 cm

subject: Physics
q: Figure represents the extension ($\Delta l$) of a wire of length $1$ meter, suspended from the ceiling of the room at one end with a load $W$ connected to the other end. If the cross-sectional area of the wire is $10^{-5}$ m$^2$ then the Young's modulus of the wire is __________ N/m$^2$.
question: Figure represents the extension ($\Delta l$) of a wire of length $1$ meter, suspended from the ceiling of the room at one end with a load $W$ connected to the other end. If the cross-sectional area of the wire is $10^{-5}$ m$^2$ then the Young's modulus of the wire is __________ N/m$^2$.
solution:
From the given graph, we can observe that for a load $W = 20$ N, the extension is $\Delta l = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

The formula for Young's modulus $Y$ is:

$Y = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{W / A}{\Delta l / L} = \dfrac{W L}{A \Delta l}$

Given:
Length of the wire, $L = 1$ m
Cross-sectional area, $A = 10^{-5}$ m$^2$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$Y = \dfrac{20 \times 1}{10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}$

$Y = \dfrac{20}{2 \times 10^{-9}}$

$Y = 10 \times 10^9 = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$

Answer: $1.0 \times 10^{10}$

subject: Physics
q: A steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>Y</mi><mi>S</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> and a copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>Y</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> , both of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> are connected end to end. When stretched by a load, the net elongation is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The load applied, in Newton, will be: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
question: A steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>Y</mi><mi>S</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> and a copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>Y</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> , both of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> are connected end to end. When stretched by a load, the net elongation is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The load applied, in Newton, will be: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
solution:
When two wires are connected end to end, the tension in them will be same.

Change in length using Hooke's law can be found as,

Y=FlA∆l⇒Δl=FlAY

Total change in length can be written as,

Δl=Δl1+Δl2   

⇒Δl=Fl1 A1Y1+Fl2 A2Y2

⇒F=Δll1 A1Y1+l2 A2Y2=1.4×10-33.2π1.4×10-32×2.0×1011+4.4π1.4×10-32×1.1×1011⇒F=1.54×102=154 N

subject: Physics
q:
One end of a metal wire is fixed to a ceiling and a load of 2 kg hangs from the other end. A similar wire is attached to the bottom of the load and another load of 1 kg hangs from this lower wire. Then the ratio of longitudinal strain of upper wire to that of the lower wire will be

[Area of cross section of wire =0.005 cm2, Y=2×1011 N m-2 and g=10 m s-2]
question:
One end of a metal wire is fixed to a ceiling and a load of 2 kg hangs from the other end. A similar wire is attached to the bottom of the load and another load of 1 kg hangs from this lower wire. Then the ratio of longitudinal strain of upper wire to that of the lower wire will be

[Area of cross section of wire =0.005 cm2, Y=2×1011 N m-2 and g=10 m s-2]
solution:
As we know, change in length is given by ΔL=FLAY

Therefore, strain required ΔLL=FAY

Now the ratio will be, ΔL1 L1ΔL2 L2=F1 F2=T1 T2=3010=3

subject: Physics
q: The bulk modulus of a liquid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The pressure required to reduce the volume of liquid by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> is :
question: The bulk modulus of a liquid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The pressure required to reduce the volume of liquid by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> is :
solution:
Bulk modulus B=Volumetric stressVolumetric strain=-∆P∆VV

Then, ∆P=B×-∆VV=3×1010×2100=6×108 N m-2

subject: Physics
q: Two wires each of radius 0.2 cm and negligible mass, one made of steel and the other made of brass are loaded as shown in the figure. The elongation of the steel wire is ______10–6 m. [Young's modulus for steel =2×1011 N m–2 and g=10 m s–2]
question: Two wires each of radius 0.2 cm and negligible mass, one made of steel and the other made of brass are loaded as shown in the figure. The elongation of the steel wire is ______10–6 m. [Young's modulus for steel =2×1011 N m–2 and g=10 m s–2]
solution:
The force acting on steel wire F is 2+1.14 g=3.14g

From the relation of Young's modulus, it can be written

FA=YΔLL

⇒ΔL=FLAY=3.14×g×1.6π×0.22×2×1011×10-4

⇒∆L=2×10-5

⇒∆L=20×10-6 m

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi></math> are the values of Young's modulus, bulk modulus and modulus of rigidity of any material respectively. Choose the correct relation for these parameters.
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi></math> are the values of Young's modulus, bulk modulus and modulus of rigidity of any material respectively. Choose the correct relation for these parameters.
solution:
Y-Young's modulus, K-Bulk modulus, η-modulus of rigidity
We know that, 

Y=3k1-2σ

⇒σ=121-Y3k           ...1

⇒Y=2η1+σ⇒σ=Y2η-1    ...2

From the equation 1 and the equation 2

121-Y3k=Y2η-1

⇒1-Y3k=Yη-2⇒Y3k=3-Yn⇒Y3k=3η-Yη

⇒ηY3k=3η-Y⇒k=ηY9η-3Y N m-2.

subject: Physics
q: The length of a metal wire is ℓ1, when the tension in it is T1 and is ℓ2 when the tension is T2. The natural length of the wire is:
question: The length of a metal wire is ℓ1, when the tension in it is T1 and is ℓ2 when the tension is T2. The natural length of the wire is:
solution:
T1=kℓ1-ℓ0

T2=kℓ2-ℓ0

T1 T2=ℓ1-ℓ0ℓ2-ℓ0

T1ℓ2-T1ℓ0= T2ℓ1-T2ℓ0

T2ℓ1-T1ℓ2 T2-T2=ℓ0

subject: Physics
q:
The fractional compression \(\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)\) of water at the depth of 2.5 km below the sea level is
______ \(\%\). Given, the Bulk modulus of water \(=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}\), density of water \(=10^3\) \(\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}\), acceleration due to gravity \(=\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\).
question:
The fractional compression \(\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)\) of water at the depth of 2.5 km below the sea level is
______ \(\%\). Given, the Bulk modulus of water \(=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}\), density of water \(=10^3\) \(\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}\), acceleration due to gravity \(=\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\).
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}\right)} \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}} \times 100=\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\mathrm{B}} \times 100 \\ & \frac{1000 \times 10 \times 2.5 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^9} \times 100 \% \\ & =1.25 \%\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The elastic limit of brass is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>379</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>. The minimum diameter of a brass rod if it is to support a <math><mn>400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> load without exceeding its elastic limit will be
question: The elastic limit of brass is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>379</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>. The minimum diameter of a brass rod if it is to support a <math><mn>400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> load without exceeding its elastic limit will be
solution:
Stress =FA
Elastic limit is 379 MPa
⇒379×106=400A=400πd2/4
d=400×4π×379×106
=1.16×10-3 m
=1.16 mm

subject: Physics
q: The increase in the pressure required to decrease the volume $(\Delta V)$ of water is $6.3 \times 10^7$ N/m$^2$. The percentage decrease in the volume is _____. (Bulk modulus of water $= 2.1 \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$.)
question: The increase in the pressure required to decrease the volume $(\Delta V)$ of water is $6.3 \times 10^7$ N/m$^2$. The percentage decrease in the volume is _____. (Bulk modulus of water $= 2.1 \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$.)
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by $B = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$

The fractional decrease in volume is $-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{B}$

Substituting the given values:

$-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{6.3 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-2}$

The percentage decrease in volume is $\left(-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \times 100$

Percentage decrease $= 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 3\%$

Answer: $3\%$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped rigidly at one end. When the other end of the wire is pulled by a force<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>f</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. Another wire of same material of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math> is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>f</mi></math>. Then the increase in its length will be:
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped rigidly at one end. When the other end of the wire is pulled by a force<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>f</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. Another wire of same material of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math> is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>f</mi></math>. Then the increase in its length will be:
solution:
Young's modulus is same for both the wires since the material is same.

For the first wire,

Y=fLπr2l   ...(i)

For the second wire,

Y=2f(2L)4πr2l'   ...(ii)

Dividing (i) by (ii)

fLπr2l×4πr2l'4fL=1⇒l'=l

subject: Physics
q:
A metal block of mass m is suspended from a rigid support through a metal wire of diameter 14 mm. The tensile stress developed in the wire under equilibrium state is 7×105 N m–2. The value of mass m is ______ kg.

(Take g=9.8 m s-2 and π=227)
question:
A metal block of mass m is suspended from a rigid support through a metal wire of diameter 14 mm. The tensile stress developed in the wire under equilibrium state is 7×105 N m–2. The value of mass m is ______ kg.

(Take g=9.8 m s-2 and π=227)
solution:
The data given is

 T=7×105 N m-2r=7×10-3 m

Using the equation

 mgA=T⇒mg=TA⇒mg=Tπr2

⇒mg=7×105×227×72×10-6

⇒mg=49×2210

⇒m=49×2298=11 kg

subject: Physics
q: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm, respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm, respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Young's modulus: $Y = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L/L}$.
When the same force is applied and both wires stretch by the same extension $\Delta L$, the ratio of Young's moduli is:
$\frac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \frac{F/A_A}{\Delta L/L_A} \times \frac{\Delta L/L_B}{F/A_B} = \frac{A_B}{A_A} \times \frac{L_A}{L_B}$
$=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} \times \frac{6.0}{5.4} = 1.5 \times \frac{10}{9} = \frac{5}{3}$.
Therefore $x : 3 = 5 : 3$, giving $x = 5$.
